planting.note: Direct sow in early spring or fall in compacted, poor, lean, disturbed soil in full sun to partial shade; pineapple weed is a pioneer annual that naturally colonizes path edges, gravel, and disturbed ground. It self-sows freely and typically requires no deliberate sowing once established in appropriate conditions.

directions: Scatter seeds on compacted, lean, disturbed, sunny to partly shaded soil and allow to naturalize; pineapple weed is a small annual of the chamomile tribe, native to northeastern Asia but widely naturalized as a weed of paths, roadsides, and disturbed ground throughout North America. Unlike true chamomile, the tiny dome-shaped flower heads lack white ray petals — only the central yellow-green disc flowers are present, giving the plant a distinctive button-like appearance. When crushed, the entire plant releases an intense, sweet pineapple-chamomile fragrance that is unmistakable and charming. The flower heads can be brewed into a pleasant, mild, pineapple-scented herbal tea similar to chamomile tea but with a tropical twist. The plant typically colonizes compacted, poor soils where it often grows in path edges, gravel driveways, and worn turf without any encouragement. Gather the button-like heads for tea when fully yellow-green and fragrant.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in dry, sandy, lean, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; American pennyroyal is a native annual of dry, open, sandy or rocky soils that self-sows freely in appropriate conditions. It was widely used by Native Americans and colonial settlers as an insect repellent and medicinal herb.

directions: Direct sow in lean, sandy or rocky, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade after last frost; American pennyroyal is a small, aromatic native annual in the mint family, distinct from the European pennyroyal (Mentha pulegium) but similarly pungently mint-scented with a sharp, camphor-like quality. Indigenous peoples throughout eastern North America used the plant extensively as an insect repellent (rubbing the leaves on skin to repel mosquitoes and flies), a medicinal herb for colds and fevers, and a culinary flavoring. The plant self-sows freely in dry, lean, open, sandy conditions and can be left to naturalize in appropriate sandy garden areas. Caution: do not use medicinally in large amounts, especially during pregnancy, as concentrated pulegone-containing plants can be toxic in excess. For fragrance and insect-repellent purposes, moderate use is traditional and appropriate.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in well-drained, dry, rocky, slightly alkaline to neutral, lean soil in full sun; southern agastache grows naturally on dry, rocky, calcareous slopes and canyon walls of the Chihuahuan Desert and the Trans-Pecos of Texas and is adapted to extreme heat and drought.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, rocky, slightly alkaline to neutral, lean soil in full sun; southern agastache is a compact hyssop-mint native to dry, rocky, calcareous slopes of the Chihuahuan Desert region in west Texas and adjacent New Mexico and Mexico. The small but attractive rosy-pink to salmon-red flower spikes are produced from midsummer through fall and are magnets for hummingbirds and large bumblebees throughout the blooming period. More drought-tolerant than most agastaches and adapted to the thin, rocky, alkaline soils of the Edwards Plateau and Trans-Pecos, making it an excellent choice for dry, rocky, calcareous gardens in zone 9. Unlike many agastaches that decline in the summer heat and humidity of the Gulf South, southern agastache thrives in the hot, dry conditions of west Texas and is more durable in drier zone 9 gardens than humid-adapted species.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in very well-drained, dry, rocky, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; littleleaf sumac is native to dry, rocky, calcareous soils of the Chihuahuan Desert and Edwards Plateau and is among the most drought-tolerant native shrubs of the Southwest. Once established, it requires no supplemental irrigation.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, dry, rocky, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; littleleaf sumac is a small-leaved, drought-adapted native shrub of the Chihuahuan Desert and Edwards Plateau of west and central Texas, recognized by its unusually tiny compound leaves with small, dark green, heat-tolerant leaflets. White flowers in early spring before leaf emergence are followed by small, sticky, tart red berries that are edible as a lemonade-like beverage (native peoples soaked them in water to make a refreshing drink, similar to other Rhus species). The persistent small red fruits are important food for mockingbirds, bluebirds, and other desert birds through summer. Outstanding fall color — brilliant orange-red — followed by attractive winter structure. An extremely drought and heat tolerant native shrub for xeric, rocky, alkaline gardens in central and west Texas and adjacent areas.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants or root divisions in spring in well-drained, average to poor, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; smooth sumac spreads aggressively by root suckers to form large colonies and should be sited where this spreading habit is acceptable or where it can be managed by periodic mowing at the colony perimeter.

directions: Plant in well-drained, average to poor, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; smooth sumac is the most cold-hardy North American sumac, naturally colonizing roadsides, old fields, and dry slopes throughout most of the continent. It is distinguished from staghorn sumac (R. typhina) by its smooth stems — staghorn has velvety, fuzzy new growth resembling a stag's antler in velvet. The brilliant, fire-engine-red fall color of smooth sumac is among the most spectacular of any native shrub, often turning color before most other plants. The large, upright, cone-shaped clusters of fuzzy, dark red berries persist through winter and are critically important food for numerous birds including robins, catbirds, mockingbirds, and cedar waxwings. Spreads by root suckers to form thicket colonies — an excellent native for erosion control on dry slopes but needs containment near formal gardens.
